hero.h1: Freelander Engines — The Complete UK Guide
hero.subHeadline: Every generation. Every engine code. Every known failure. Every replacement cost — including the honest answer about when replacement no longer makes sense. Backed by 279+ real UK Freelander 2 enquiries [EM-VERIFIED].

metrics.ourCall: ⚠️→❌ Frequently Not Worth a Full Recon — a £2,800–£4,500 reconditioned engine against £2,000–£7,000 host values fails our 60% scrap-threshold rule on most cars. A good used engine (£800–£2,000) is the realistic route on this model.
★★★☆☆ — the Freelander 2's 2.2 diesel is genuinely dependable; the Freelander 1's petrol engines are where the reputation was damaged
2.2 TD4/SD4 (224DT) — Freelander 2 • Td4 BMW M47 — Freelander 1
1.8 K-series — the head-gasket engine that built the Freelander's unfair reputation
2011–2014 Freelander 2 SD4 — facelift car, strongest engine, last of the line

ownershipVerdict.oneLineVerdict: The Freelander is the one Land Rover where our job is often to talk you out of a full engine replacement — the 2.2 diesel is worth saving, but on this model the maths matters more than the mechanicals.

generations.subHeadline: Two generations with almost nothing in common under the bonnet. Select yours.

cards.title: Freelander 1
cards.verdict: Britain's best-selling 4x4 of its day, undone by the 1.8 petrol's head gasket. The BMW-engined Td4 diesel is the one to have — the rest are now labour-of-love territory.

cards.title: Freelander 2 (L359)
cards.verdict: A quietly excellent car — the 2.2 diesel is one of the most dependable engines Land Rover ever fitted. The buying risk is the car's age and value, not the engine.

comparisonLink.label: Read the full comparison: Freelander 1 vs Freelander 2 →

engineDatabase.h2: Freelander Engine Codes — The Complete Database

confidenceScore.title: How confident are these ratings?
confidenceScore.text: 224DT rating: 90% confidence — 279 model-level L359 enquiries [EM-VERIFIED] plus its long, well-documented service history across Freelander 2, Evoque L538 and early Discovery Sport. All Freelander 1 and petrol FL2 ratings: Expert-knowledge tier — the parc is old enough that documented failure patterns are extensive, but our proprietary volume data doesn't reach back that far. Data note (internal): Correction applied: the source data sheet listed "2.0 Ingenium (204DTD — late L359)" as a Freelander engine. The Freelander never received an Ingenium engine — L359 production ended in 2014 and the 204DTD launched in 2015 with the Discovery Sport/Evoque. Removed, and the missing genuine engines added: 1.8 K-series, L-series, BMW Td4 and KV6 (FL1); Volvo SI6 and Si4 GTDi petrols (FL2). If the source data contains "204DTD Freelander" enquiries, they are almost certainly misattributed Discovery Sport records — worth cleaning at source.

problems.description: The failure that defined the Freelander 1. Coolant loss, overheating, mayonnaise under the oil cap. A quality multi-layer-steel gasket and revised rail fix it permanently — prevention beats replacement on this engine every time.
The Freelander 2 diesel's most common failure enquiry [EM-VERIFIED]. Rough running, smoke, uneven idle. Repairable at sensible cost if addressed before bore wash sets in.
Urban-use Freelander 2 diesels clog DPFs — repeated regenerations dilute the oil and mask deeper issues. A motorway run is maintenance on this engine.
The Freelander 1 diesel's known weak points — split intercooler hoses mimic turbo failure; genuine turbo wear appears at high mileage. Usually repairable; rarely worth a full engine.
Three belts, tight service access, and head gaskets of its era. Belt neglect is terminal — and on today's values, terminal means scrap.
Not an engine failure, but the most common non-engine enquiry on the L359 — driveline vibration and binding often misdiagnosed as engine or gearbox trouble.

marketIntelligence.h2: Freelander Market Intelligence — What 279 UK Owners Told Us in 2025

signals.data: The dominant share of all Freelander enquiries [EM-VERIFIED]
Not captured — parc largely below repair-economics threshold

insights: Interesting insight: A model that ended production in 2014 still generates 279 annual engine enquiries — more than the Velar. The Freelander 2's parc is ageing, but its owners are choosing to keep them alive.

Freelander 2 enquiries skew toward repair-vs-replace decisions rather than pre-purchase checks — the signature of a model at the bottom of its value curve, where every engine issue is an economic crossroads. (Editorial synthesis — specialist sense-check before publishing.)
We expect used-engine demand to outstrip recon demand on this model — host values simply can't carry full reconditioning costs. (Editorial synthesis.)

editorialPullQuote.title: The One Thing We'd Tell Every Freelander Owner
editorialPullQuote.quote: If you own a Freelander 2 with a tired engine, price a good used 2.2 before you price a recon — on this model the maths usually points there. And if you own a 1.8 Freelander 1 that hasn't had the head gasket done properly: do it before it does you.

replacementCosts.h2: Freelander Engine Replacement Costs — By Generation
replacementCosts.subHeadline: Real UK pricing — with the used-engine route given equal billing, because on this model it's usually the right answer.
replacementCosts.figuresNote: All figures [PLACEHOLDER — confirm vs Table A; Freelander rows were not in the original cost sheet].
replacementCosts.labourEstimate: Fitting a Freelander engine typically takes 8–16 hours at £65–£95/hr (independent) or £110–£160/hr (London/SE dealer) [EM-QUOTE]. The Freelander rule — the inverse of the Defender: On most Freelanders, a full recon fails our 60% scrap-threshold rule: £3,500 of engine in a £4,000 car is not a repair, it's a donation. The honest hierarchy on this model is (1) repair the fault, (2) fit a good used engine, (3) recon only on the best late SD4s, (4) walk away with our blessing. We'll tell you which applies to yours — for free.

engineEvolution.h2: How the Freelander Engine Range Evolved — 1997 to 2014

eras.whyBmwChanged: The Freelander was built from the Rover Group parts bin — light, cheap, and the K-series' cooling margins didn't survive 4x4 duty
eras.worthKnowing: The L-series diesel is the forgotten good engine of early Freelanders

eras.whyBmwChanged: BMW ownership brought a properly engineered diesel — the M47 transformed the Freelander's credibility
eras.worthKnowing: The Td4 is the only Freelander 1 we'd still recommend today

eras.whyBmwChanged: Ford ownership rebuilt the Freelander from scratch on the EUCD platform — Duratorq diesel, Volvo straight-six petrol
eras.worthKnowing: The 2.2 Duratorq went on to power the Evoque and launch-era Discovery Sport — its parts network is enormous

eras.whyBmwChanged: The Freelander name retired; the Discovery Sport took its place and its customer
eras.worthKnowing: Early Discovery Sports even kept the Freelander's 2.2 engine for the first year

profiles.verdict: A late Freelander 2 SD4 is honest, spacious family transport at banger money — go in with a £1,000 contingency fund and eyes open.
Motorway commuters suit the 2.2 diesel well; short urban runs are DPF poison. Town-only drivers should consider the SI6 petrol despite the fuel bills.
2,000kg braked on the FL2 — genuinely useful for trailers and small caravans. This is the cheapest tow-capable Land Rover on the road.
This is the Freelander's home turf — the cheapest route into Land Rover ownership. The discipline: never spend more on one repair than the car is worth.
Forgiving to own, cheap to insure, every fault documented a thousand times over. A well-bought Freelander 2 is a great first 4x4; a cheap 1.8 FL1 is a great first lesson.

items.question: How much does a Freelander engine replacement cost in the UK?
items.answer: A used engine typically costs £400–£2,000 supply only; reconditioned units run £1,500–£4,500 depending on engine [PLACEHOLDER — confirm vs Table A]. Fitting adds £520–£1,300. On most Freelanders, the used route is the economically sensible one. Use our calculator for a personalised verdict.

items.question: Which Freelander engine is the most reliable?
items.answer: The 2.2 TD4/SD4 (224DT) in the Freelander 2 — one of the most dependable engines Land Rover ever fitted. On the Freelander 1, the BMW-sourced Td4 is comfortably the pick.

items.question: Which Freelander engine should I avoid?
items.answer: The 1.8 K-series petrol — unless the head gasket has already been done properly with a multi-layer-steel gasket, in which case the hard part is behind it. The 2.5 KV6 demands cambelt discipline that rarely survives banger-money ownership.

items.question: Is the Freelander head gasket problem fixable?
items.answer: Yes, permanently — a quality MLS gasket, revised oil rail and proper coolant bleeding resolve it for good. At £600–£1,200 it's the one big-ticket repair on a Freelander 1 that can still make sense. Prevention beats replacement on this engine.

items.question: Is it worth replacing a Freelander engine?
items.answer: Honestly: often not with a full recon. Most Freelanders fail our 60% scrap-threshold rule for reconditioned engines — but a good used 2.2 at £800–£2,000 in a solid late Freelander 2 is a sound decision. Use the Ownership Decision Tool for your car.

items.question: Which Freelander should I buy?
items.answer: A 2011–2014 Freelander 2 SD4 with history — facelift car, strongest engine, last of the line. Among Freelander 1s, only the Td4 diesel, and only cheap.

items.question: How do I find my Freelander engine code?
items.answer: On the VIN plate and vehicle documentation; block stampings vary by engine family. Or tell us your variant and year (TD4, SD4, 1.8, KV6) and we'll identify it.

items.question: Freelander 2 vs Discovery Sport — should I upgrade?
items.answer: The Discovery Sport is the Freelander 2's direct successor — early ones even share the 2.2 engine. Upgrade for the extra seats and refinement; stay if your Freelander 2 is healthy, because a good one owes you nothing. Read the full head-to-head →
